stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{\left(z - ([[p]])\right)\left(z - ([[q]])\right)} \), which has simple poles at \( z = [[p]] \) and \( z = [[q]] \). Let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing both poles. Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: By the residue theorem, \( \oint_{C} f\,dz = 2\pi i\sum \operatorname{Res} \). The residues at the two simple poles sum to \( \dfrac{([[a]])([[p]])+([[b]])}{([[p]])-([[q]])} + \dfrac{([[a]])([[q]])+([[b]])}{([[q]])-([[p]])} = [[a]] \), so the integral is \( 2\pi i\,([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the residue at \( z = [[p]] \); the theorem sums the residues at all enclosed poles.
This uses only the residue at \( z = [[q]] \); the theorem sums the residues at all enclosed poles.
This is the sum of the residues but omits the factor \( 2\pi i \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \).
This has the wrong sign (contour orientation reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{\left(z - ([[p]])\right)\left(z - ([[q]])\right)} \), with simple poles at \( z = [[p]] \) and \( z = [[q]] \). Let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \), counting only the poles that actually lie inside \( C \).
solution: Only \( z=[[p]] \) satisfies \( |z| < [[R]] \) (since \( |[[q]]| > [[R]] \), the pole at \( z=[[q]] \) lies outside \( C \)). So \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i\operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f = 2\pi i\cdot\dfrac{([[a]])([[p]])+([[b]])}{([[p]])-([[q]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums the residues at both poles, but the pole at \( z=[[q]] \) lies outside \( |z|=[[R]] \) and must be excluded.
This uses only the residue at the pole \( z=[[q]] \), which is not even enclosed by \( C \).
The integral is not \( 0 \); the pole at \( z=[[p]] \) is genuinely enclosed by \( C \).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{\left(z - ([[p]])\right)\left(z - ([[q]])\right)} \), with simple poles at \( z = [[p]] \) and \( z = [[q]] \). Let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: Both \( |[[p]]| \) and \( |[[q]]| \) exceed \( [[R]] \), so neither pole lies inside \( C \); \( f \) is analytic on and inside \( C \), so by Cauchy's theorem \( \oint_C f\,dz = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the residue-theorem factor \( 2\pi i \) even though no pole is actually enclosed.
This applies the residue-theorem factor with the wrong sign, and no pole is actually enclosed anyway.
This treats the numerator's leading coefficient as if it were a residue; no pole is enclosed, so the integral is \( 0 \).
This treats the numerator's constant term as if it were a residue; no pole is enclosed, so the integral is \( 0 \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of recognizing that no pole is enclosed.
The integral of an analytic function over a closed contour with no interior singularities is \( 0 \), not \( 1 \).

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{\left(z - ([[pr]]+[[pim]]i)\right)\left(z - ([[qr]]+[[qim]]i)\right)} \), which has simple poles at \( z = [[pr]]+[[pim]]i \) and \( z = [[qr]]+[[qim]]i \) (neither pole is real). Let \( C \) be a positively oriented rectangle whose interior contains both poles. Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: By the residue theorem, \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i\sum \operatorname{Res} \), summing the residues at both enclosed poles even though they are off the real axis: \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=p}f + \operatorname{Res}_{z=q}f \), where \( p=[[pr]]+[[pim]]i,\ q=[[qr]]+[[qim]]i \). Multiplying by \( 2\pi i \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
